locked_facts: Convex lens has positive focal length
Object beyond 2F: real, inverted, diminished image between F and 2F
Object between F and 2F: real, inverted, magnified image beyond 2F
Object within F: virtual, erect, magnified image on same side as object
Object at infinity: image at F (focal point)
Power P = 1/f (dioptre), positive for convex lens
truth_statements: A convex lens is thicker at the centre and converges light rays
When an object is placed beyond 2F, the image is real, inverted and diminished
When an object is placed between F and the optical centre, the image is virtual, erect and magnified
The focal length of a convex lens is positive according to the new Cartesian sign convention
The lens formula relates object distance, image distance and focal length

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show object at F — all three rays diverge after the lens, never meeting. The image is at infinity. Then move object slightly inside F — rays diverge on the right but their backward extensions converge on the LEFT, forming a virtual image. The convex lens that was forming real inverted images now forms a virtual erect magnified image. Same lens, different position, completely different image.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Smooth transition of object from beyond 2F through 2F, between F and 2F, to within F. Watch the image flip from real-inverted to virtual-erect as the object crosses F.

simulation_states.label: Object beyond 2F — real, inverted, diminished
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Object arrow on the far left. Three standard rays drawn. Real inverted diminished image between F and 2F on the right.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: When the object is far from the lens, the image is smaller than the object and inverted.

simulation_states.label: Object between F and 2F — real, inverted, magnified
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Object moved closer, between F and 2F. Image is now beyond 2F on the other side — bigger and inverted.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: As the object moves closer to F, the image moves farther away and becomes larger.

simulation_states.label: Object within F — virtual, erect, magnified
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Object placed between F and the lens. Rays diverge after the lens. Virtual image (dotted) on the same side as the object — erect and magnified.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: This is the magnifying glass effect. The image is virtual because the refracted rays never actually converge — your brain traces them back to form a virtual image.

simulation_states.label: Interactive — move the object
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Slider to adjust object distance. Image position, size, and nature update in real-time.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Experiment: move the object across F and watch the image flip from real to virtual.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: A convex lens always magnifies objects — it always makes things look bigger
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: A convex lens magnifies only when the object is within the focal length (magnifying glass mode). When the object is beyond 2F, the image is actually diminished (smaller). Between F and 2F, it is magnified but inverted.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show object beyond 2F with diminished image. Then move within F to show magnification. The lens behaviour depends entirely on where the object is placed relative to F.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: A convex lens always forms a real image because it converges light
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: A convex lens forms a virtual image when the object is placed within the focal length. The refracted rays diverge and never actually meet — the virtual image is formed by backward extension of rays.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Demonstrate the transition: object beyond F gives real image (rays converge), object within F gives virtual image (rays diverge, dotted extensions show virtual image).
